symbols.name: \nu
symbols.def: Common dominating measure for the full-data classes.

symbols.name: O_i
symbols.def: Observed unit \(i\), with treatment and outcome components \(A_i\) and \(Y_i\).

symbols.name: \mu
symbols.def: Common dominating measure for observed-data laws.

symbols.name: e_P
symbols.def: A fixed Borel version of \(P(A=1\mid X=\cdot)\).

symbols.name: m_{P,a}
symbols.def: A fixed Borel version of \(E_P[Y\mid A=a,X=\cdot]\), for \(a\in\{0,1\}\).

symbols.name: s_0
symbols.def: Hölder smoothness of the exact-tie stratum.

symbols.name: L_0
symbols.def: Hölder radius of the exact-tie stratum.

symbols.name: s_1
symbols.def: Hölder smoothness of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: L_1
symbols.def: Hölder radius of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: \alpha
symbols.def: Margin exponent of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: C
symbols.def: Margin constant of the near-tie stratum.

symbols.name: \beta
symbols.def: Fixed boundary power used in the sparse near-tie bump.

symbols.name: C_n
symbols.def: A sample-measurable, almost surely finite connected closed interval \(C_n=[L_n,U_n]\) with \(L_n\le U_n\).

symbols.name: \psi
symbols.def: Boundary-vanishing reference bump on \( [0,1]^d\), extended by zero outside that cube.

symbols.name: h_n
symbols.def: \(h_n=c_h n^{-2/D}\), where \(c_h>0\) is a fixed sufficiently small constant.

symbols.name: \mathcal C_n
symbols.def: The lexicographically first \(N_n\) pairwise disjoint cubes of side \(h_n\) lying in \( [1/4,3/4]^d\).

symbols.name: c_{\ell,n}
symbols.def: Lower-left corner of the \(\ell\)-th cube in \(\mathcal C_n\).

symbols.name: \theta
symbols.def: Rademacher sign vector indexing a sparse near-tie law.

symbols.name: \Delta_{\theta,n}
symbols.def: Sparse signed contrast on the cubes \(\mathcal C_n\).

symbols.name: P_0^\circ
symbols.def: Observed image of the zero-mean randomized Rademacher full-data law in \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\).

symbols.name: K
symbols.def: Finite constant in the sparse-mixture chi-square bound.

symbols.name: c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}
symbols.def: Positive constant depending only on \(\gamma\) and \(\varepsilon\).

symbols.name: C_n^\dagger
symbols.def: Connected interval sequence associated with the union-calibrated route.

novel.justification: An exact-treatment-response tie stratum describes settings in which either treatment has the same conditional mean for every baseline profile; it is a law-level benchmark for the exceptional boundary.

standard.name: Tsybakov margin condition with an allowed tie atom

definitions.name: admissible ordered pair
definitions.construction: Write \(s=r+\tau\) with \(r=\lfloor s\rfloor\) and \(\tau\in[0,1)\); \(C^s(\mathcal X)\) has bounded continuous derivatives through order \(r\), with order-\(r\) derivatives \(\tau\)-Hölder when \(\tau>0\). Fix \(0<s_1<s_0\), \(L_0,L_1>0\), and \(\alpha,C>0\) satisfying \(s_1\alpha<1\), and choose \(\beta\in(s_1,1/\alpha)\).

definitions.name: exact-tie base law
definitions.construction: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, and conditionally independently draw \(Y(0),Y(1)\in\{-B,B\}\) with conditional means zero.  Let \(P_0^\circ\) be its observed image.

definitions.name: sparse randomized-sign near-tie family
definitions.construction: For each sufficiently large \(n\), use \(\mathcal C_n\) and \(\Delta_{\theta,n}\). Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, set \(E_F[Y(1)\mid X=x]=\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\) and \(E_F[Y(0)\mid X=x]=-\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\), and conditionally independently draw \(Y(a)\in\{-B,B\}\) with the corresponding Rademacher probabilities.  The constants \(c_h,c_a,c_N\) are fixed small enough that every displayed mean lies in \([-\bar B,\bar B]\), every conditional variance is at least \(v_0\), and the Hölder and margin bounds hold.

definitions.name: sequence-level honest adaptation criterion
definitions.construction: For every positive deterministic \(a=(a_n)_n\), use \(\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) and \(\mathfrak R^{\mathrm{asy}}_j(a)\) exactly as defined in the symbol table; honesty is a property of the entire interval sequence.

definitions.name: union-calibrated upper-construction handle
definitions.construction: Construct a connected interval by fitting multiscale contrast estimates on training folds, using independent holdout folds to form simultaneous confidence bounds for their dyadic block coefficients, and calibrating the final interval to the maximum smoothing and nuisance bias over both \(\mathcal F_0\) and \(\mathcal F_1\).

statements.statement: Under \(\mathrm{ass:consistency}\), \(\mathrm{ass:exchangeability}\), \(\mathrm{ass:randomization}\), \(\mathrm{ass:bounded-outcomes}\), and \(\mathrm{ass:variance-floor}\), consistency, conditional exchangeability, and positivity from known randomization identify \(m_{P,a}(x)=E_F[Y(a)\mid X=x]\) for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\) and each \(a\in\{0,1\}\); consequently \(|m_{P,a}(x)|\le\bar B=\sqrt{B^2-v_0}\) for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\).
statements.justification: Identification precedes the conditional second-moment calculation that yields the envelope.
statements.gap: Both identification and the envelope are standard supporting facts, not contributions.
statements.consumer: It supplies the outcome slack used by the sparse family.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix \(a\in\{0,1\}\).  By consistency, on \(\{A=a\}\) one has \(Y=Y(a)\).  Conditional exchangeability and the positivity \(P(A=a\mid X)=1/2\) supplied by known randomization therefore give, for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\),
\[
 m_{P,a}(x)=E_P[Y\mid A=a,X=x]
 =E_F[Y(a)\mid A=a,X=x]
 =E_F[Y(a)\mid X=x].
\]
This proves the asserted identification without defining the causal quantity to be the observed regression.

By bounded outcomes, \(E_F[Y(a)^2\mid X=x]\le B^2\).  The conditional variance identity and \(\mathrm{ass:variance-floor}\) now imply
\[
 v_0\le \operatorname{Var}_F(Y(a)\mid X=x)
 =E_F[Y(a)^2\mid X=x]-E_F[Y(a)\mid X=x]^2
 \le B^2-m_{P,a}(x)^2.
\]
Consequently \(m_{P,a}(x)^2\le B^2-v_0=\bar B^2\), and hence \(|m_{P,a}(x)|\le\bar B\), for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\).

statements.statement: The base law in \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\) belongs to \(\mathcal F_0\).  For every admissible pair, the zero-contrast law also belongs to \(\mathcal F_1\); hence both observed-data strata and \(\mathcal U\) are nonempty.
statements.justification: A union-honesty problem is meaningful only for compatible dominated full-data classes.
statements.gap: This is a model audit rather than a claimed advance over optimal-value procedures.
statements.consumer: It validates the ordered pair used in the lower-bound calculation.
statements.proof_tex:
Let \(F^\circ\) denote the full-data law specified in \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\). Because \(X\) is uniform on \(\mathcal X=[0,1]^d\), \(A\) has mass \(1/2\) at each point of \(\{0,1\}\), and each potential outcome is supported on the two atoms \(\{-B,B\}\), the product construction gives \(F^\circ\ll\nu\). Indeed, relative to the factors of \(\nu=\lambda_d\otimes\#_{\{0,1\}}\otimes\kappa_B\otimes\kappa_B\), the corresponding factor densities are respectively \(1\), \(1/2\), and, for each potential outcome, \(1/2\) on each of the atoms \(-B\) and \(B\) and zero elsewhere.

The observation map gives \(Y=Y(A)\), so consistency holds. The conditional mean-zero requirement and the support \(\{-B,B\}\) force
\[
F^\circ\{Y(a)=B\mid X,A\}=F^\circ\{Y(a)=-B\mid X,A\}=\frac12,
\qquad a\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Thus the conditional law of \((Y(0),Y(1))\) given \((X,A)\) does not depend on \(A\), which proves \((Y(0),Y(1))\perp A\mid X\). Independence of \(A\) and \(X\) gives \(e_{P_0^\circ}(x)=1/2\), while uniformity of \(X\) gives \(g_{P_0^\circ}(x)=1\), in each case for \(\lambda_d\)-almost every \(x\). Moreover, \(|Y(a)|=B\) almost surely and
\[
\operatorname{Var}_{F^\circ}(Y(a)\mid X)=B^2\ge v_0,
\]
where the last inequality follows from \(v_0\in(0,B^2)\).

For each \(a\in\{0,1\}\), consistency and the preceding conditional distribution yield
\[
m_{P_0^\circ,a}(x)=E_{P_0^\circ}[Y\mid A=a,X=x]=0
\]
for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\). Hence \(\lVert m_{P_0^\circ,a}\rVert_{C^{s_0}(\mathcal X)}=0\le L_0\), and \(\Delta_{P_0^\circ}=0\) almost everywhere. All defining conditions in \(\mathrm{def:exact-tie-stratum}\) therefore hold, so \(F^\circ\in\mathcal F_0\).

Now fix any admissible pair from \(\mathrm{def:admissible-pair}\) and regard the same \(F^\circ\) as the zero-contrast law. The domination, consistency, exchangeability, randomization, boundedness, variance, and density calculations above are unchanged. Since the zero function belongs to every Hölder class with norm zero,
\[
\max_{a\in\{0,1\}}\lVert m_{P_0^\circ,a}\rVert_{C^{s_1}(\mathcal X)}=0\le L_1.
\]
For every \(u\in(0,2\bar B]\), the event \(\{0<|\Delta_{P_0^\circ}(X)|\le u\}\) is empty, and consequently
\[
P_X\{0<|\Delta_{P_0^\circ}(X)|\le u\}=0\le Cu^\alpha.
\]
Thus every defining condition in \(\mathrm{def:near-tie-stratum}\) holds and \(F^\circ\in\mathcal F_1\).

Finally, by \(\mathrm{def:observed-strata}\), the observed image \(P_0^\circ=T_\#F^\circ\) belongs to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\). Hence both observed-data strata are nonempty, and therefore their union \(\mathcal U=\mathcal P_0\cup\mathcal P_1\) is nonempty.

statements.statement: For every \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), \(E_P[\widehat V_{0,n}]=V(P)\), and \(P^n\{V(P)\in C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}}\}\ge1-\gamma\).  Consequently \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_0}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}})]=O(n^{-1/2})\).
statements.justification: When the contrast is identically zero, the value is an ordinary randomized-arm mean.
statements.gap: This is the oracle benchmark, not a novelty claim; Luedtke--van der Laan already motivate regular versus exceptional optimal-value inference.
statements.consumer: It defines the root-\(n\) precision that union honesty may fail to retain at the exact-tie stratum.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix \(P\in\mathcal P_0\).  The exact-tie condition gives \(m_{P,1}(X)=m_{P,0}(X)\) almost surely, and thus
\[
 V(P)=E_P[\max\{m_{P,0}(X),m_{P,1}(X)\}]=E_P[m_{P,0}(X)].
\]
Randomization and iterated expectation yield
\[
 E_P[2\,1\{A=0\}Y\mid X]
 =2P(A=0\mid X)E_P[Y\mid A=0,X]
 =m_{P,0}(X).
\]
Averaging over \(X\) proves \(E_P[\widehat V_{0,n}]=V(P)\).

Set \(Z_i=2\,1\{A_i=0\}Y_i\).  Under iid sampling, \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n\) are independent, have common mean \(V(P)\), and lie in \([-2B,2B]\).  Hoeffding's inequality, in its bounded-range form,
\[
 P^n\!\left\{\left|n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i-E_PZ_i\right|\ge t\right\}
 \le 2\exp\!\left\{-\frac{2n t^2}{(4B)^2}\right\}
 =2\exp\!\left\{-\frac{nt^2}{8B^2}\right\},
\]
with \(t=B\sqrt{8\log(2/\gamma)/n}\), gives
\[
 P^n\{V(P)\in C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}}\}\ge1-\gamma.
\]
Finally, the interval length is nonrandom and equals
\[
 2B\sqrt{8\log(2/\gamma)/n}.
\]
Taking the supremum over \(P\in\mathcal P_0\) proves the claimed \(O(n^{-1/2})\) bound.

statements.statement: For all sufficiently large \(n\) and every \(\theta\in\{-1,1\}^{N_n}\), \(F_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal F_1\), \(P_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal P_1\), and \(V(P_{\theta,n})-V(P_0^\circ)=\delta_n\).  Moreover \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\).
statements.justification: The signed construction keeps the causal max functional positive while the signs conceal the alternatives from product testing.
statements.gap: Audibert--Tsybakov give classification-margin constructions, but not this full-data randomized causal max-functional family with an exact-tie oracle stratum.
statements.consumer: It supplies the explicit cross-stratum alternatives for the adaptation lower bound.
statements.proof_tex:
Write \(s=s_1\), let
\[
 J_1=\int_{[0,1]^d}\psi(z)\,dz>0,
 \qquad
 J_2=\int_{[0,1]^d}\psi(z)^2\,dz>0,
\]
and extend \(\psi\) by zero as in the symbol declaration.  Since \(\beta>s\), every derivative required by the definition of \(C^s\) vanishes continuously at the boundary of the cube, and the order-\(\lfloor s\rfloor\) derivatives have the required Hölder modulus.  Consequently there is a finite constant \(M_\psi\), depending only on \((d,s,\beta)\), such that for every \(h\in(0,1)\), every collection of disjoint cubes \(Q_\ell=c_\ell+h[0,1]^d\), and every signs \(\vartheta_\ell\in\{-1,1\}\),
\[
 \left\|h^s\sum_\ell \vartheta_\ell
 \psi\!\left(\frac{\cdot-c_\ell}{h}\right)\right\|_{C^s(\mathcal X)}
 \le M_\psi.
\]
Indeed, scaling gives this bound within one cube.  Between two cubes, the boundary vanishing of all derivatives through order \(\lfloor s\rfloor\) bounds the cross-cube Hölder quotient by the same constant.  Disjointness ensures that no point receives two nonzero summands.

We next verify the margin inequality rather than treating it as part of the construction.  For \(z\in[0,1]\), put \(r(z)=2\min\{z,1-z\}\).  Then \(4z(1-z)\ge r(z)\), and \(r(Z)\) is uniform on \([0,1]\) when \(Z\) is uniform.  Iterated integration gives, for independent uniform \(R_1,\ldots,R_d\) and \(q\in(0,1]\),
\[
 \Pr\!\left\{\prod_{j=1}^dR_j\le q\right\}
 =q\sum_{k=0}^{d-1}\frac{\{\log(1/q)\}^k}{k!}.
\]
Because \(\alpha\beta<1\), the right-hand side at \(q=t^{1/\beta}\) is at most \(M_{\alpha,\beta,d}t^\alpha\), uniformly over \(t\in(0,1]\).  The same bound, after increasing the constant, is trivial for \(t\ge1\).  Hence
\[
 \lambda_d\{z\in(0,1)^d:0<\psi(z)\le t\}
 \le M_{\alpha,\beta,d}t^\alpha,
 \qquad t>0.
\]
Since the cubes are disjoint and \(X\) is uniform under the construction,
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 P_X\{0<|\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)|\le u\}
 &=N_nh_n^d\lambda_d\{0<\psi\le u/a_n\}\\
 &\le M_{\alpha,\beta,d}N_nh_n^d(u/a_n)^\alpha\\
 &\le M_{\alpha,\beta,d}c_Nc_a^{-\alpha}u^\alpha.
 \end{aligned}
\]
Thus choosing the fixed calibration so that \(M_{\alpha,\beta,d}c_Nc_a^{-\alpha}\le C\) proves \(\mathrm{ass:margin-near-tie}\).

Now consider the full-data law in \(\mathrm{def:sparse-near-tie-family}\).  Uniform \(X\), randomized \(A\), and endpoint-supported potential outcomes give domination by \(\nu\), consistency under the observation map, and conditional exchangeability because \(A\) is independent of \((X,Y(0),Y(1))\).  They also give \(g_P=1\), \(e_P=1/2\), and bounded outcomes.  The observed regressions are
\[
 m_{P_{\theta,n},1}(x)=\frac{\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)}2,
 \qquad
 m_{P_{\theta,n},0}(x)=-\frac{\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)}2.
\]
The scaling bound above gives
\[
 \max_{a\in\{0,1\}}\|m_{P_{\theta,n},a}\|_{C^s(\mathcal X)}
 \le \frac{M_\psi c_a}{2}.
\]
Choose \(c_a\le2L_1/M_\psi\).  Also \(\|\Delta_{\theta,n}\|_\infty\le a_n\to0\), so eventually \(|\Delta_{\theta,n}|/2\le\bar B\).  For the endpoint Rademacher outcome with mean \((2a-1)\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\),
\[
 \operatorname{Var}_F(Y(a)\mid X=x)
 =B^2-\frac{\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)^2}{4}
 \ge B^2-\bar B^2=v_0.
\]
All conditions defining \(\mathcal F_1\) now hold, so \(F_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal F_1\), and its observed image belongs to \(\mathcal P_1\).

The base law has both regressions equal to zero, hence \(V(P_0^\circ)=0\).  Under an alternative,
\[
 \max\{m_{P_{\theta,n},0}(x),m_{P_{\theta,n},1}(x)\}
 =\frac{|\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)|}{2}.
\]
The signs disappear after taking the absolute value, and a change of variables on every cube gives
\[
 V(P_{\theta,n})-V(P_0^\circ)
 =\frac12\int_{\mathcal X}|\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)|\,dx
 =\frac{a_nN_nh_n^dJ_1}{2}
 =\delta_n.
\]
Finally, \(s\alpha<1\le d\), so \(h_n^{s\alpha-d}\to\infty\) and
\[
 N_n\sim c_Nh_n^{s\alpha-d}.
\]
Therefore
\[
 \delta_n\asymp h_n^s h_n^{s\alpha-d}h_n^d
 =h_n^{s(\alpha+1)}
 \asymp n^{-2s(\alpha+1)/(d+s\alpha+4s)}
 =n^{-\xi}.
\]

statements.statement: For every admissible pair, there are fixed admissible choices of \(c_h,c_a,c_N\) and \(\varepsilon\in(0,1-2\gamma)\) such that \(\chi^2(\overline Q_n,(P_0^\circ)^n)\le\exp\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\}-1\le\varepsilon^2\) eventually. Hence every \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) obeys \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]/\delta_n\ge c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0\). If, in addition, \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\xi<1/2\), so union honesty forces a strictly slower-than-root-\(n\) expected length at an exact-tie law even though \(\mathcal P_0\) alone admits \(O(n^{-1/2})\) length.
statements.justification: The sparse-mixture chi-square and expected-length conclusions hold for every admissible pair; \(3s_1\alpha<d\) is reserved solely for the strict slower-than-root-\(n\) comparison.
statements.gap: The explicit causal mixture establishes one component of the lower frontier but does not by itself include the parametric root-\(n\) obstruction.
statements.consumer: Together with the parametric tie lower bound, it determines the unavoidable scale faced by a union-honest clinical interval at the exact-tie law.

statements.statement: Does the union-calibrated route in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\) yield a sequence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) with \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_0}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)]=O(\delta_n)\) and the corresponding near-tie risk controlled on \(\mathcal P_1\)?
statements.justification: The proved symbol \(C_n^\dagger\) denotes the split-sample interval. The separate symbol \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) denotes the hypothetical multiscale route whose sharper rate remains open.
statements.gap: Attainment of \(O(\delta_n)\) by \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) remains open where it is not excluded by the parametric tie lower bound.
statements.consumer: A sharper answer would determine whether multiscale calibration can improve on the current conservative split-sample clinical interval.
statements.proof_tex: The explicit sequence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\) is finite-sample honest over \(\mathcal U\) and has uniform expected length \(O(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho})\) on both strata. Attainment of \(O(\delta_n)\) is impossible when \(3s_1\alpha>d\) and remains open in the nonexcluded regimes.

statements.statement: Since \(\xi-1/2=(3s_1\alpha-d)/[2(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)]\), the displayed sparse family has \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\), equal to root-\(n\) order when \(3s_1\alpha=d\) and \(o(n^{-1/2})\) when \(3s_1\alpha>d\). Is a root-\(n\) union-honest connected interval attainable when \(3s_1\alpha\ge d\), or does a different least-favorable family establish a binding obstruction there? When \(3s_1\alpha<d\), can a union-honest interval attain the proved \(n^{-\xi}\) lower scale?
statements.justification: The root-\(n\) subregime \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\) is settled by matching lower and upper scales, while the remaining regimes have an explicit honest bracket.
statements.gap: For \(2s_1\alpha<d\), neither the split-sample upper construction nor the two current lower experiments closes the exponent gap.
statements.consumer: The open regimes specify where a new interval construction or a new least-favorable causal family would change practical precision guarantees.
statements.proof_tex: For both strata, the minimax expected-length coefficient is bounded below at \(\ell_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\xi\}}\) and bounded above at \(u_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\rho\}}\). The scales match at root \(n\) when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\); the regimes \(2s_1\alpha<d\le3s_1\alpha\) and \(3s_1\alpha<d\) remain open.

statements.statement:
There is an explicit cellwise local-polynomial estimator, based on \(m\) iid training observations and smoothness \(s_1\), whose induced rule \(\widehat d_m(x)=1\{\widehat m_1(x)\ge\widehat m_0(x)\}\) satisfies
\[
 \sup_{P\in\mathcal P_1}E_{P^m}\!\left[V(P)-E_P\{m_{P,\widehat d_m(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d_m\}\right]
 \le K m^{-s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)}.
\]
The constant \(K\) depends only on the frozen class parameters.  For every \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), the regret inside the expectation is identically zero for every training realization.
statements.proof_tex:
Put \(s=s_1\), \(q=\lceil s\rceil-1\), and let \(\phi(u)=(u^k:|k|\le q)\) be the finite vector of tensor monomials on \([0,1]^d\).  Let
\[
 k_m=\left\lceil m^{1/(2s+d)}\right\rceil,
 \qquad h_m=k_m^{-1},
\]
and partition \(\mathcal X\) into the \(k_m^d\) cubes of side \(h_m\).  If \(Q\) has lower corner \(c_Q\), write \(\phi_Q(x)=\phi((x-c_Q)/h_m)\) on \(Q\).  For \(a\in\{0,1\}\), define
\[
 G_{a,Q}=\frac2m\sum_{i=1}^m1\{A_i=a,X_i\in Q\}\phi_Q(X_i)\phi_Q(X_i)^\top,
\]
\[
 H_{a,Q}=\frac2m\sum_{i=1}^m1\{A_i=a,X_i\in Q\}Y_i\phi_Q(X_i).
\]
Let \(M_\phi=\int_{[0,1]^d}\phi(u)\phi(u)^\top du\) and \(\lambda_\star=\lambda_{\min}(M_\phi)/4>0\).  If \(\lambda_{\min}(G_{a,Q})\ge\lambda_\star h_m^d\), set \(\widehat b_{a,Q}=G_{a,Q}^{-1}H_{a,Q}\); otherwise set \(\widehat b_{a,Q}=0\).  For \(x\in Q\), define \(\widehat m_a(x)\) by truncating \(\phi_Q(x)^\top\widehat b_{a,Q}\) to \([-\bar B,\bar B]\).  This is a completely sample-measurable estimator.

We first establish its pointwise tail bound uniformly over \(P\in\mathcal P_1\).  Randomization gives
\[
 E_PG_{a,Q}=\int_Qg_P(x)\phi_Q(x)\phi_Q(x)^\top dx.
\]
The density lower bound implies
\[
 \lambda_{\min}(E_PG_{a,Q})
 \ge\frac12h_m^d\lambda_{\min}(M_\phi)
 =2\lambda_\star h_m^d.
\]
For every \(m_{P,a}\in C^s(\mathcal X)\), Taylor's formula on a cube gives a polynomial \(p_{a,Q}\) of degree at most \(q\) satisfying
\[
 \sup_{x\in Q}|m_{P,a}(x)-p_{a,Q}(x)|\le K_1h_m^s.
\]
For integer \(s\), this is the degree-\((s-1)\) Taylor bound using the bounded order-\(s\) derivatives; for noninteger \(s\), it is the degree-\(\lfloor s\rfloor\) Taylor bound using the stated Hölder modulus.  The constants are uniform because the Hölder radius is \(L_1\).

Let \(b^*_{a,Q}\) be the population weighted least-squares coefficient.  The preceding approximation, the density bounds, and the population Gram lower bound imply
\[
 \sup_{x\in Q}|m_{P,a}(x)-\phi_Q(x)^\top b^*_{a,Q}|\le K_2h_m^s,
 \qquad \|b^*_{a,Q}\|\le K_2.
\]
For independent bounded variables, scalar Bernstein's inequality reads
\[
 \Pr\{|m^{-1}\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^m(Z_i-EZ_i)|>t\}
 \le2\exp\!\left\{-\frac{mt^2}{2(\sigma^2+Mt/3)}\right\}
\]
when \(|Z_i-EZ_i|\le M\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(Z_i)\le\sigma^2\).  Apply this inequality to the finitely many entries of \(G_{a,Q}\) and to the coordinates of
\[
 \frac2m\sum_{i=1}^m1\{A_i=a,X_i\in Q\}
 \phi_Q(X_i)\{Y_i-\phi_Q(X_i)^\top b^*_{a,Q}\}.
\]
Each summand is bounded by a class-dependent constant and has variance at most a constant times \(h_m^d\).  A finite-dimensional union bound, the population eigenvalue bound, and the inverse perturbation identity therefore give constants \(K_3,k_3>0\) such that, for every fixed \(x\in Q\),
\[
 P^m\{|\widehat m_a(x)-m_{P,a}(x)|>K_3(h_m^s+u)\}
 \le K_3\exp(-k_3mh_m^du^2)+K_3\exp(-k_3mh_m^d)
\]
for \(0<u\le1\).  Truncation cannot increase the error because the true regression lies in \([-\bar B,\bar B]\).

Set
\[
 r_m=h_m^s+(mh_m^d)^{-1/2}\asymp m^{-s/(2s+d)}.
\]
The last display and a union bound over the two arms imply, uniformly in \(x\), a sub-Gaussian bound for \(|\widehat\Delta(x)-\Delta_P(x)|/r_m\) on its bounded range.  If \(\widehat d_m(x)\) chooses the wrong sign and \(\Delta_P(x)\ne0\), then
\[
 |\widehat\Delta(x)-\Delta_P(x)|\ge|\Delta_P(x)|.
\]
Split the nonzero contrast range into \(0<|\Delta_P|\le K_4r_m\) and the dyadic shells
\[
 2^jK_4r_m<|\Delta_P|\le2^{j+1}K_4r_m,
 \qquad j\ge0.
\]
The first region contributes at most \(K r_m^{\alpha+1}\) by the margin condition.  On shell \(j\), the pointwise tail bound and the margin condition give a contribution at most
\[
 K r_m^{\alpha+1}2^{j(\alpha+1)}e^{-k4^j}.
\]
The series over \(j\) is finite; the exponentially small Gram-failure term is smaller than every fixed power of \(r_m\).  Fubini's theorem consequently yields
\[
 \sup_{P\in\mathcal P_1}E_{P^m}
 E_{P_X}\!\left[|\Delta_P(X)|1\{\widehat d_m(X)\ne1\{\Delta_P(X)\ge0\}\}\mid\widehat d_m\right]
 \le K r_m^{\alpha+1}.
\]
The inner expectation is exactly the policy-value regret, with zero contribution from the tie set.  Since \(r_m^{\alpha+1}\asymp m^{-s(\alpha+1)/(2s+d)}\), the claimed near-tie bound follows.  On \(\mathcal P_0\), \(m_{P,0}=m_{P,1}\) almost surely, so every rule has the same value and the regret is identically zero.

statements.statement:
There is a constant \(c_\gamma>0\) such that every \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) satisfies
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}n^{1/2}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]\ge c_\gamma.
\]
The base law \(P_0^\circ\) belongs to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\), so this lower bound applies to both stratum-wise frontier criteria.
statements.proof_tex:
Choose a fixed \(t>0\), to be made small below, and put \(\mu_n=t/\sqrt n\).  Define a full-data law \(F_{t,n}\) exactly as in the base construction except that, conditionally independently for \(a\in\{0,1\}\), the endpoint-supported potential outcome has mean \(E[Y(a)\mid X]=\mu_n\).  Thus
\[
 F_{t,n}\{Y(a)=B\mid X\}=\frac{1+\mu_n/B}{2},
 \qquad
 F_{t,n}\{Y(a)=-B\mid X\}=\frac{1-\mu_n/B}{2}.
\]
For all sufficiently large \(n\), these laws are dominated by \(\nu\), have uniform design density and propensity one half, satisfy consistency and exchangeability, have constant regressions \(m_0=m_1=\mu_n\), and obey
\[
 \operatorname{Var}(Y(a)\mid X)=B^2-\mu_n^2\ge v_0.
\]
Their constant regression norms are eventually below both \(L_0\) and \(L_1\), and the margin event \(\{0<|\Delta|\le u\}\) is empty.  Hence their observed images \(P_{t,n}\) belong to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\), while
\[
 V(P_{t,n})-V(P_0^\circ)=\mu_n.
\]

Under \(P_0^\circ\), the one-observation likelihood ratio is
\[
 \frac{dP_{t,n}}{dP_0^\circ}(O)=1+\frac{\mu_nY}{B^2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
 1+\chi^2(P_{t,n}^n,(P_0^\circ)^n)
 =\left(1+\frac{\mu_n^2}{B^2}\right)^n
 \le\exp(t^2/B^2).
\]
Choose \(t>0\) so small that
\[
 \rho_t:=\frac12\sqrt{e^{t^2/B^2}-1}<1-2\gamma.
\]
Then the total variation distance between the two product laws is at most \(\rho_t\).

For any union-honest interval sequence, uniform honesty applied to the base and the triangular sequence \(P_{t,n}\) gives coverage at least \(1-\gamma-r_n\) for some \(r_n\downarrow0\).  Transferring the alternative coverage event to the base law and intersecting it with base coverage yields
\[
 (P_0^\circ)^n\{0\in C_n,\ \mu_n\in C_n\}
 \ge1-2\gamma-2r_n-\rho_t.
\]
Connectedness forces length at least \(\mu_n\) on this event.  Consequently
\[
 \liminf_n n^{1/2}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]
 \ge t(1-2\gamma-\rho_t)=:c_\gamma>0.
\]
The final stratum-wise assertion follows from \(\mathrm{lem:nonempty-strata}\), which places \(P_0^\circ\) in both observed strata.

statements.statement:
Let \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\). There is an explicit split-sample connected interval sequence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) such that
\[
 \sup_{P\in\mathcal P_j}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)]
 \le K_\dagger\{n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\},
 \qquad j\in\{0,1\},
\]
for a finite constant \(K_\dagger\) depending only on the frozen class parameters and \(\gamma\). Consequently, for \(u_n=n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\), \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\le K_\dagger<\infty\) for both \(j=0\) and \(j=1\).
statements.proof_tex:
Let \(m_n=\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) and \(q_n=n-m_n\). On the first \(m_n\) observations, construct the explicit local-polynomial rule \(\widehat d_{m_n}\) from \(\mathrm{lem:local-polynomial-policy-regret}\). On the remaining observations define
\[
 \widehat W_n
 =\frac1{q_n}\sum_{i=m_n+1}^n
 2\,1\{A_i=\widehat d_{m_n}(X_i)\}Y_i.
\]
Let \(K_{\mathrm{reg}}\) be the uniform constant in that lemma, set
\[
 \rho=\frac{s_1(\alpha+1)}{2s_1+d},
 \qquad
 b_n=\frac{2K_{\mathrm{reg}}}{\gamma}m_n^{-\rho},
 \qquad
 t_n=B\sqrt{\frac{8}{\gamma q_n}},
\]
and define the connected interval
\[
 C_n^\dagger=[\widehat W_n-t_n,\ \widehat W_n+t_n+b_n].
\]
This is a sample-measurable, almost surely finite closed interval.

Condition on the training sample, and abbreviate \(\widehat d=\widehat d_{m_n}\). Randomization gives
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 E_P[2\,1\{A=\widehat d(X)\}Y\mid X,\widehat d]
 &=m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X),\\
 W_P(\widehat d)
 &:=E_P[m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d].
 \end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(E_P[\widehat W_n\mid\widehat d]=W_P(\widehat d)\). Each validation summand lies in \([-2B,2B]\), so its conditional variance is at most \(4B^2\). Conditional independence of the validation observations and Markov's inequality applied to the squared centered mean yield
\[
 P^n\{|\widehat W_n-W_P(\widehat d)|>t_n\mid\widehat d\}
 \le\frac{4B^2}{q_nt_n^2}=\frac\gamma2.
\]

The policy regret
\[
 R_P(\widehat d)=V(P)-W_P(\widehat d)
 =E_P\!\left[\max_{a\in\{0,1\}}m_{P,a}(X)-m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d\right]
\]
is nonnegative. If \(P\in\mathcal P_1\), \(\mathrm{lem:local-polynomial-policy-regret}\) and Markov's inequality give
\[
 P^{m_n}\{R_P(\widehat d)>b_n\}
 \le\frac{K_{\mathrm{reg}}m_n^{-\rho}}{b_n}
 =\frac\gamma2.
\]
If \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), the same lemma states that \(R_P(\widehat d)=0\) for every training realization. On the intersection of the validation event \(|\widehat W_n-W_P(\widehat d)|\le t_n\) and the training event \(R_P(\widehat d)\le b_n\),
\[
 \widehat W_n-t_n\le W_P(\widehat d)\le V(P)
 \le W_P(\widehat d)+b_n\le\widehat W_n+t_n+b_n.
\]
A union bound therefore shows, for every \(n\ge2\),
\[
 \inf_{P\in\mathcal U}P^n\{V(P)\in C_n^\dagger\}\ge1-\gamma.
\]
Hence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\).

The interval length is deterministic and equals
\[
 \operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)=2t_n+b_n.
\]
Since \(m_n\) and \(q_n\) are each bounded below by a positive multiple of \(n\) for \(n\ge2\), there is a finite \(K_\dagger\), depending only on \(B,\gamma,K_{\mathrm{reg}},s_1,\alpha,d\), such that
\[
 2t_n+b_n\le K_\dagger\{n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\}.
\]
This bound is uniform over both strata. Inserting this single data-driven interval sequence into the infimum defining \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\) proves the final assertion. Notice that this construction uses no dependency on the multiscale handle in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\).
statements.justification: The theorem supplies one explicit connected interval, constructed from a learned policy and an independent randomized evaluation sample, with finite-sample union coverage.
statements.gap: Its regret allowance yields \(n^{-\rho}\), which is generally larger than the sparse lower scale \(n^{-\xi}\).
statements.consumer: It gives an immediately implementable conservative route for the rilpivirine-versus-efavirenz optimal-value analysis.

statements.statement:
Let
\[
 \ell_n=n^{-1/2}\vee\delta_n,
 \qquad
 \rho=\frac{s_1(\alpha+1)}{2s_1+d},
 \qquad
 u_n=n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}.
\]
There are constants \(0<c_*\le K_*<\infty\), depending only on the frozen class parameters and \(\gamma\), such that for both \(j\in\{0,1\}\),
\[
 \mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(\ell)\ge c_*,
 \qquad
 \mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\le K_*.
\]
Moreover,
\[
 \ell_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\xi\}},
 \qquad
 u_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\rho\}},
 \qquad
 \rho<\xi.
\]
Thus the bracket collapses to the root-\(n\) scale when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\). When \(2s_1\alpha<d\le3s_1\alpha\), it ranges from the lower scale \(n^{-1/2}\) to the achievable scale \(n^{-\rho}\); when \(3s_1\alpha<d\), it ranges from the lower scale \(n^{-\xi}\) to the achievable scale \(n^{-\rho}\).
statements.justification: The theorem combines the parametric tie experiment, the sparse randomized-sign experiment, and the explicit split-sample interval into one actionable two-sided rate bracket.
statements.gap: The bracket is matched only when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\); its tightness remains unknown in the two nonparametric regimes.
statements.consumer: The bracket gives downstream analysts a uniformly honest interval scale now and identifies exactly where sharper theory could improve expected length.

sampling_model.overlap: The propensity is fixed at \(1/2\) almost everywhere.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: The lower-bound family is fully specified and uses no fitted nuisance; the upper construction is an open multiscale sample-splitting route.

bibliography.citation: A. R. Luedtke and M. J. van der Laan (2016). Statistical inference for the mean outcome under a possibly non-unique optimal treatment strategy. \textit{Annals of Statistics} 44, 713--742. doi:10.1214/15-AOS1384.
P. Wu and L. Wang (2021). Inference for optimal treatment regimes. \textit{Biometrics} 77, 465--476. doi:10.1111/biom.13337.

C. Shi, W. Lu, and R. Song (2020). Breaking the curse of nonregularity with subagging: Inference of the mean outcome under optimal treatment regimes. \textit{Journal of Machine Learning Research} 21(176), 1--67.
S. Xu and X. Guo (2026). Optimal inference of the mean outcome under optimal treatment regime. \textit{arXiv:2509.09773}.
J. Whitehouse, Q. Chen, M. Austern, and V. Syrgkanis (2025). Inference on optimal policy values and other irregular functionals via softmax smoothing. \textit{arXiv:2507.11780}.
K. Ponomarev and V. Semenova (2024). Inference on optimal welfare. \textit{arXiv:2410.07443}, revised September 2025.
R. Liu, Z. Liu, Y. Sasaki, and Y. Wan (2025). Nonparametric classification and policy value inference. \textit{arXiv:2511.14700}.
T. T. Cai and M. G. Low (2004). An adaptation theory for nonparametric confidence intervals. \textit{Annals of Statistics} 32, 1805--1840. doi:10.1214/009053604000000049.
T. B. Armstrong and M. Kolesar (2020). Simple and honest confidence intervals in nonparametric regression. \textit{Quantitative Economics} 11, 1--39. doi:10.3982/QE1199.
J.-Y. Audibert and A. B. Tsybakov (2007). Fast learning rates for plug-in classifiers. \textit{Annals of Statistics} 35, 608--633. doi:10.1214/009053606000001217.

W. Liu, Z. Zhang, L. Nie, and G. Soon (2017). A case study in personalized medicine: Rilpivirine versus efavirenz for treatment-naive HIV patients. \textit{Journal of the American Statistical Association} 112, 1381--1392. doi:10.1080/01621459.2017.1280404.

comparator_promise_table.comparator_claim: Proposition 1 gives a standard expected-confidence-length lower-bound reduction from testing pairs for intervals honest over a union.
The article constructs bias-aware honest intervals under specified smoothness restrictions and emphasizes limits on honest data-driven adaptation.
The article characterizes exceptional-law nonregularity and develops optimal-value inference under sufficient conditions.
The article develops nominal-coverage subagging intervals for nonregular optimal values and compares their average lengths.
The article gives smoothed robust and resampling-based inference for an optimal-regime parameter and value.
The preprint develops adaptive smoothing and an asymptotic-variance bound within a robust asymptotically linear unbiased estimator class.
The preprint develops constant-fit softmax-smoothed optimal-policy-value inference and reports efficiency when treatment nonresponse is zero.

The preprint constructs uniformly valid one-sided lower confidence bands robust to non-unique policies through moment-inequality tests and separation or margin considerations, but not the present two-sided connected interval, union-honest expected-length lower bound, or sparse-mixture construction.

The preprint proves Gaussian optimal-policy-value inference using a strictly convex surrogate under distinct representative-policy assumptions, but not the exact-tie ordered union or its two-sided union-honest expected-length lower bound.

tldr: Under iid sampling, known one-half randomization, bounded outcomes, Hölder outcome regressions, and a margin condition that permits a tie atom, a sparse randomized-sign experiment forces every union-honest connected interval for the optimal policy value to have expected length at least \(n^{-1/2}\vee n^{-\xi}\) at the exact-tie base law, where \(\xi=2s_1(\alpha+1)/(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)\). The explicit split-sample learned-policy interval \(C_n^\dagger\) is uniformly honest and has length \(O(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho})\), where \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\). These scales coincide at root \(n\) when \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\); outside that subregime, attainment of the sparse lower scale by the distinct hypothetical multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) remains open.

project_justification.gap: Existing inference for exceptional optimal-treatment laws does not provide this two-stratum expected-length adaptation diagram for a connected interval that is honest simultaneously over exact ties and Hölder near ties with an allowed tie atom. The unresolved issue is sharply localized: when \(2s_1\alpha<d\), the proved lower exponent \(\min\{1/2,\xi\}\) and achievable exponent \(\min\{1/2,\rho\}\) differ.
project_justification.niche: The note studies honest adaptation for the causal optimal-policy value, taking its randomized-trial identifying functional as given. Its distinctive ingredients are an explicit dominated sparse causal mixture for the lower bound and a single data-driven split-sample learned-policy interval for the upper bound.
project_justification.fill: The lower calculation holds for every admissible pair; the inequality \(3s_1\alpha<d\) is needed only for the corollary that the sparse scale is strictly slower than root \(n\). Combining that calculation with a parametric tie submodel gives the unified lower scale \(n^{-1/2}\vee n^{-\xi}\) on both strata. The proved split-sample interval \(C_n^\dagger\) supplies the honest upper scale \(n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\); the symbol \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) is reserved for the unresolved multiscale route.

related_work: Luedtke--van der Laan and subsequent work analyze inference at exceptional laws, while policy-learning results supply regret control under smoothness and margin conditions. Cai--Low-style testing arguments explain how indistinguishable alternatives constrain expected interval length, and Armstrong--Kolesar emphasize the cost of honest bias control under predeclared restrictions. Ponomarev--Semenova and Liu--Liu--Sasaki--Wan address related non-uniqueness-robust or representative-policy inference problems. None of those results supplies the present explicit sparse randomized-sign causal product experiment together with this exact-tie/near-tie union bracket. The current result is a proved lower/upper bracket, not a claim of a globally matched frontier.
interpretation: At an exact biomarker-response tie, an analysis restricted to the tie stratum can use a root-\(n\) interval, but a procedure that must also cover nearby heterogeneous treatment effects can be forced to be wider. For individualized rilpivirine-versus-efavirenz regime analysis, the result separates three facts: the unavoidable lower cost, the usable uniformly honest split-sample interval \(C_n^\dagger\), and the remaining mathematical gap represented by the hypothetical multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\). The proved interval evaluates a policy learned on an independent training half and adds an explicit allowance for its worst-case regret.
technical_internal_limitation: The split-sample construction converts an expected local-polynomial policy-regret bound into coverage by Markov calibration. This produces the exponent \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\), which is strictly smaller than \(\xi\) under the admissibility condition. Thus \(C_n^\dagger\) attains the unified lower scale only in the root-\(n\) subregime \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\). When \(2s_1\alpha<d\), it is unknown whether a fully specified multiscale interval \(C_n^{\mathrm{ms}}\) with sharper high-probability regret or bias control improves the upper rate, or whether a different causal least-favorable experiment strengthens the lower bound.
honest_scope: The sampling model is iid observed-data sampling from a randomized binary-treatment law with known propensity \(1/2\), bounded potential outcomes, a conditional-variance floor, and a design density bounded above and below. The union contains an exact-tie Hölder stratum and a near-tie Hölder stratum satisfying the written margin inequality while allowing an arbitrary tie atom. The conclusions concern connected two-sided intervals for the identified optimal-policy value. They do not cover observational propensity estimation, unknown smoothness or margin parameters beyond this frozen pair, nonconnected confidence sets, or a globally sharp adaptation frontier in the open regimes.
